headline: Suspect in BGC shooting involved in POGOs, Gatchalian says | ABS-CBN News
text:
The Chinese suspect in the BGC shooting incident early this week is part of a syndicate that has links to Philippine Offshore Gaming Operators (POGO), Senator Win Gatchalian said Saturday.
Gatchalian told Teleradyo Serbisyo that the information was disclosed to him by law enforcement sources.
“Confirmed na itong tao ay konektado sa POGO. At nakapasok siya dito dahil sa POGO. Kung matatandaan natin yung mga unang panahon, napakabilis magtayo ng POGO, napakabilis magtrabaho sa POGO, kaya sinu-sino na lang nakakapagtrabaho sa POGO,” Gatchalian said.
The suspect, according to the senator, is a “remnant” of a kidnapping syndicate in the country.
Senators want POGO workers' employment permits revoked, reviewed
The suspect, he added, is part of the group that is involved in a recent crime.
“Ngayon, ang balita ko dito sa shooting incident, ito yung mga sindikato na naiwan na dito. At itong taong ito ay kabahagi ng mga nagkidnap. At may mga ebidensya rin na nagtuturo na itong taong ito, kasama rin doon sa mga, kung matatandaan mo, may isang news report na lumabas na may pinagutan ng ulo. Kasama siya dito,” Gatchalian said.
“Kaya nung lumabas yung pag-aresto sa kanya, naglaban siya. Makikita naman natin. Ang nakakatakot, itong mga taong ito, nakatira sa mga subdivision, high-end villages. Sa BGC ito eh na kung saan maraming mga turista, maraming mga negosyante ang nakatira,” he added.
Gov't 'fixers' may be behind foreign POGO workers' PH IDs: official
Gatchalian has been actively pushing for a ban on POGOs in the country.
His committee report on the issue has yet to be discussed in the Senate plenary.
Gatchalian warned the public that members of POGO syndicates are now using legitimate government IDs like PhilHealth and driver’s license.
“Yung POGO talagang, ano ito eh, talagang nakakasira sa ating lipunan dahil hanggang ngayon, nakikita pa rin natin itong mga problema nito... Yung Bugatti nga, yung dalawang Bugatti sa Alabang na nakuha, POGO rin ang inpormasyon ko. POGO rin ang may-ari nitong Bugatti. Dahil sino namang lokal ang makaka-afford ng P180 milyon na sasakyan? At balita namin, POGO rin ito. Kaya hanggang ngayon, hindi lang lumalabas sa media. Yung iba ay sulpot-sulpot lang sa media. Pero nandito pa rin yung kanilang ginagawang hindi magaganda,” he said.
It was also Gatchalian who revealed that two smuggled Bugatti Chiron cars, said to be worth P170 million a piece, are also owned by POGOs.
RELATED VIDEO

headline: A love of sharks brings women together at a lab in Florida | ABS-CBN News
text:
Nestled within the University of Miami’s Rosenstiel School of Marine, Atmospheric, and Earth Science, overlooking the serene expanse of Biscayne Bay, lies a laboratory led by Dr. Catherine Macdonald.
As the director of the university’s Shark Research and Conservation Program, Dr. Macdonald spearheads research on shark habitats - while emphasizing science education for young girls.
"One of the most meaningful parts of being a woman in science is the work that my lab does to support girls in STEM and their interest," Dr. Macdonald said. "I get letters from young girls who are excited about shark science, sometimes just asking scientific questions, sometimes asking how they can grow up to be like me."
Each week, Dr. Macdonald and her team embark on research expeditions aboard their vessel, collecting samples and data vital to understanding the dynamics of Biscayne Bay's ecosystem. "The samples that we took help us answer questions about how this ecosystem is functioning now," Dr. Macdonald said. "But they will also help us answer questions about how it's changing in the face of the human-caused alterations."
Among Dr. Macdonald's students is her newest intern, Haley Aker, an undergraduate student whose lifelong passion for marine science and sharks drew her to the lab. Aker's journey reflects a common narrative of perseverance among aspiring female scientists.
"I applied to numerous shark labs and tried to get my foot in the door, by meeting with other shark professors and just trying to get my name out there… But it seemed that they only were selecting other males to be their volunteers and their helpers,” she said. “Finally, being able to come here and be given this opportunity to work with sharks and, you know, work on my dream has been awesome. And I love that I'm finally able to, like, prove myself."
Through her commitment to conservation and her love of sharks, Dr. Macdonald is nurturing a new generation of scientists poised to confront the complexities of marine conservation. And they in term serve as a source of inspiration for her.
"When they ask me how I don't lose hope,” Dr. Macdonald said, “because conservation is sometimes a happy experience, but often a sad and challenging one. Where would I get the nerve to give up if they're not?" -- Report from Reuters.
(Production: Maria Alejandra Cardona and Natalie Thomas)

headline: Ilang pamilya apektado sa sunog sa Pasay City | ABS-CBN News
text:
MAYNILA — Sumiklab ang sunog sa isang residential area sa Apelo Cruz Street sa Barangay 157 sa Pasay City, nitong Biyernes ng gabi.
Umabot ng ikalawang alarma ang sunog na nagsimula bandang 8:30 p.m. 
Idineklara itong fire out ng Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP) ng 10:27 p.m. Nasa 3 hanggang 5 bahay ang natupok, dagdag ng BFP. 
Walang sugatan o nasawi sa sunog. 
“One of the challenges ng responders natin ay ang pagpasok sa mismong involved na mga bahay na nasusunog. Mga eskenita ito at mostly ang kaya lang magsabay papasok ay mga 3 [o] 2 tao lang,” ani SFO1 Rezmond Germino, public information officer ng BFP-Pasay.
“Yung mga mabilis na pagkalat ng apoy din dahil mostly light materials [na] construction materials lang ang ginamit sa bahay,” dagdag ni SFO1 Germino.
READ: Fire hits neighborhood in Malibay, Pasay
Sa inisyal na listahan ng barangay, 35 na pamilya o 128 na indibidwal ang apektado.
Habang sa inisyal na tala ng BFP-Pasay, nasa 8 hanggang 10 pamilya ang apektado at nasa 3 hanggang 5 mga bahay ang natupok.
Napaluha ang residenteng si Joey Ponce, habang kinukuwento ang sinapit ng kanyang 4 na palapag na bahay at computer shop. 
Bagamat naisalba niya ang karamihan sa computer sets, malaking bahagi ng kanyang shop ang nasunog. Inutang pa umano niya ang kapital dito noong binuksan 3 taon na ang nakalipas.
“Sa awa ng Diyos buhay kami. Masakit sa kalooban na masunugan. Iniisip ko na kunin ‘yung mga computer dahil wala namang kukuha kundi kami lang. Baka mapakinabangan pa,” aniya.
Humihingi si Ponce ng tulong para mapagawa muli ang kanilang bahay.
Sabi ng isa sa mga nasunugan na si Marides Fabilla, mabilis lumaki ang apoy.
Kuwento niya, damit at dokumento lamang ang kanyang naisalba.
“Nakaligtas naman kami, awa ng Diyos. Buti na lang ganoon. Di baleng masunog ang gamit, mabibili pa yan pero yung mga apo ko una kong niligtas,” dagdag niya.
Pansamantalang nananatili sa isang open court ang mga pamilyang nasunugan. Nangako rin ang barangay ng tent para sa mga residente.
Nagsimula na ring maglista ang mga kawani ng Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) para mamigay ng tulong sa mga nasunugan.
SUNOG SA QUEZON CITY
Nasunog naman ang bahagi ng isang bahay sa Barangay Bagbag sa Novaliches, Quezon City nitong Sabado ng madaling araw.
Sabi ng Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP) - Quezon City, nagsimula ang sunog sa laundry room ng dalawang palapag na bahay bandang 12:57 ng madaling araw. Itinaas agad ito sa unang alarma matapos ang 6 na minuto.
Mabilis na naapula ang sunog kaya idineklara ng BFP-QC ang fire out ng 1:18 a.m.
Sabi ni SFO4 Christopher Navarro, ang fire ground commander, walang tao nang mangyari ang sunog sa bahay, pero nai-report agad ito ng mga kapitbahay. 
“Naka-firewall naman lahat ng area natin,” ani SFO4 Navarro.
KAUGNAY NA VIDEO

headline: Fil-Ams weigh in on Biden-Trump rematch after Super Tuesday | ABS-CBN News
text:
Former U.S. president Donald Trump has secured enough delegates to be the likely Republican nominee for the November elections, facing once again with Joe Biden for the White House.
Trump’s last remaining rival, former South Carolina governor Nikki Haley dropped out of the race a day after the Super Tuesday primaries.
Trump hails Super Tuesday wins as Haley to drop out
Filipino-American Kristine Caalim, who voted for Haley said she was disappointed at the results of the primary.  But she added that she is not ready to switch her vote to Biden.
“It's gonna be a hard sell,” she said. “Like, we need to focus on our problems at home. And that's where my heart is.”
A recent poll by the New York Times and Siena College shows that 48% of registered voters would vote for Trump if asked to cast their ballot now, while 43% would choose Biden.  
But political observers like Joshua Ang Price believe there is still room for Biden to win Haley's supporters to his side.
“I've spoken to many, many Republicans that they said they were tired of Donald Trump,” said Price, secretary of the Democratic Party of Arkansas. “I've overheard conversations like, I liked Donald Trump's platform. I voted for him the first time, but the way he conducted himself, the way he acted, that's not how I would like my children to act.”
Price, who ran as secretary of state in Arkansas in 2022, added that there is a real opportunity for the Biden campaign to reach out to undecided voters.
“In states like Arkansas and middle America, it really has to do with just grassroots engagement.,” he said. “That type of strategy would really serve Biden well.”
He added: “If you already have a certain group that's discontent with Donald Trump, it might just be that phone call or that knock on the door from someone from the Biden campaign that can flip that voter to vote for him instead.”

headline: ‘Honorary’ SMNI chair Quiboloy has no info House panel needs — lawyers | ABS-CBN News
text:
MANILA — Kingdom of Jesus Christ leader Pastor Apollo Quiboloy is only an honorary chairman of Sonshine Media Network International does not have the information that the House Committee on Legislative Franchises has summoned him for, his lawyers said.
Lawyers Ferdinand Topacio, Joselito Lomangaya and Raphael Antonio Andrada wrote Parañaque Rep. Gus Tambunting, panel chair, saying the pastor "does not possess the specific details or documents."

They said that Quiboloy "has not been involved in the management and operations of Sonshine Media Network International since the last quarter of 2018" and since the franchise renewal in 2019.
They said the Securities and Exchange Commission had been informed of Quiboloy's replacement as executive pastor by Marlon Acobo in December 2022. The change is also reflected in the network's amended Article of Incorporation released in January 2023, they also said.
"Thereafter, the only title, (if one may call it that) that [Quiboloy] has continued to hold in SMNI is that of 'Honorary Chairman' which, as the name implies, is purely honorific in character," they told the committee.
READ: Quiboloy is ultimate beneficial owner of SMNI — lawmaker
They said the panel should instead direct its inquiries to Acobo, lawyer Eunice Ambrocio and Maria Norfelly Marimon.
"Therefore, we respectfully request that the Committee issues the corresponding subpoenae to the aforementioned individuals in the stead of [Quiboloy]," they said.
Tambunting declined to comment on the letter on Friday night but reiterated that the subpoena is "personal" to Quiboloy. 
RELATED: Why Ejercito withdrew objection to Quiboloy arrest order
"It is within the discretion of Congress to subpoena the particular persons who would best answer the questions posed in the inquiry," the panel chair said in a text message.
The committee resumes its hearing on March 12.
It is also tackling a bill revoking the congressional franchise of Swara Sug Media Corp.
The Senate is also conducting a separate investigation into allegations of abuse within the Kingdom of Jesus Christ religious group, where Quiboloy bills himself the Appointed Son of God.
RELATED: Quiboloy cited in contempt by Senate panel

headline: Coconut oil tanker accident causes spill in SLEX | ABS-CBN News
text:
A coconut oil tanker collided with a Skyway post in SLEX, leading to an oil spill. Izzy Lee, ABS-CBN News.
MANILA -- A coconut oil tanker collided with a Skyway post and led to an oil spill on the South Luzon Expressway (SLEX) in Magallanes on Saturday morning.
The slippery road conditions resulted in numerous motorcycle riders losing control.
According to the Metropolitan Manila Development Authority (MMDA) the incident was reported to them past 8:00 AM.
Kwinces Gutierrez said she sustained injuries after the motorcycle she was riding slipped and toppled on the slick road.
"Mabagal lang po 'yung pagmamaneho ni kuya.'Yung mga nasa unahan namin na naka-motor, bigla na lang siyang natumba. Sumemplang na po siya ng kusa," she said. "Kami na 'yung sumunod na sumemplang kasi mapapansin po talaga na madulas 'yung daan."
[The driving of the guy was slow. The ones in front of us who were on motorcycles fell and tumbled. We were next to slip because you can really tell that the road was slippery.]
Gutierrez highlighted the lack of precautions on the road, leading to many drivers being caught off guard and resulting in a series of accidents. 
"Wala din po kasing nakalagay na precautions or kahit anong warning, kaya hindi rin po aware yung ibang mga riders. As in, sunod-sunod po talaga yung pag ano ng mga motor," she said. 
[There were also no precautions or any warnings posted, so other riders weren't aware. It's like a series of motorcycles falling one after the other.]
The incident caused slight traffic congestion, but clearing operations were initiated and successfully completed by 11:00 AM, as confirmed by the MMDA.

headline: Palestinian cause joins Women's Day marches in Latin America | ABS-CBN News
text:
Thousands of Latin American feminists marched on Friday (March 8) on an International Women's Day that was marked by pro-Palestinian demands.
In Argentina, Palestinian flags were raised among the crowd of banners and signs as they marched to the country’s Congress, where feminists also chanted against the libertarian President, Javier Milei.
Femicides in Argentina, already at a record level last year, have increased further in the first two months of 2024, a report from La Casa del Encuentro non-governmental organization report showed in February, with 61 gender-based murders of women and girls due to their gender by the end of that month.
In Chile, women held up signs demanding a free Palestine and for President Gabriel Boric to cut ties with the Israeli state.
Women also raised their voices to shed light on femicides and gender inequalities.
In Latin America and the Caribbean, at least 4,050 women were victims of femicide according to the Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean’s latest data. -- Report from Reuters
(Production: Miguel lo Bianco, Rodrigo Gutierrez, Sebastian Rocandio, Enrique Mandujano, Nina Lopez, Alberto Fajardo, Santiago Limachi, Monica Machicao, Gloria Lopez, Anna Portella)

headline: 'Yes', wagi sa plebisito sa 3 barangay sa Marawi City | ABS-CBN News
text:
Kuha ni Merlyn Manos 
Maayos ang naging takbo ng plebisito ngayong araw sa tatlong barangay sa Marawi City.
Ang plebisito ay upang likhain ang Barangay Sultan Corobong (mula sa Barangay Dulay) Barangay Sultan Panoroganan (mula sa Barangay Kilala) at Barangay Angoyao (mula sa Barangay Patani).
Ayon sa mga botante, kahit mano-mano, madali silang nakaboto dahil dalawa lang ang pagpipilian kaya wala silang problema. 
Karamihan sa aming nakapanayam ay pabor sila sa paghati ng kanilang mga barangay dahil layunin naman ay para maayos ang serbisyo para sa kanila. 
Ayon kay Jamail Miraato, Poll to Chairman dito inumpisahan nila ng 7:30am alinsunod sa utos ng Commission on Elections.
Si Abdulbasit Ali, maaga pa lang, bumoto na. Wala umano syang nakikitang mali upang hatiin ang Barangay Dulay. 
 Nag ikot naman si Commissioner Aimee Ferolino. Aniya, naging maayos ang botohan dahil na din sa walang kumontra sa ginagawang botohan.
Ganon pa man kahit tatlong barangay lang ito buong pwersa ang COMELEC upang masiguro ang pagsasaayos ng botohan.
Bandang alas onse ng umaga sa Kilala Elementary School, marami pa ang nakapila na boboto pero maayos naman silang nag antay sa labas ng gate.
Bantay sarado ang mga  ito ng mga pulis at sundalo. Anila wala namang nagpumilit na mauna sila sa pila kahit marami sila.
Sa Patani Elementary School, kung saan marami ang boboto, mga 11:30 ng umaga ay nagpapahinga na ang electoral board dahil konti na lang ang hindi pa nakaboto.
Bandang alas-3 ng hapon ay nagsara na ang botohan.
Nanalo ang yes votes, kaya’t sa ngayon ang Marawi City ay may 101 barangay na.
 Nagpasalamat naman ang COMELEC sa PNP, AFP, lalong lalo na sa mga guro na naglingkod sa botohan at sa mga taong bumoto ngayong araw. - Ulat ni Merlyn Manos

headline: Pentagon: 'No evidence' of UFO sightings, technology | ABS-CBN News
text:
A Pentagon report on unidentified flying objects said US government investigations since the end of World War 2 have found no evidence of extraterrestrial technology and had concluded that most sightings were misidentified ordinary objects and phenomena.
The report released on Friday (March 8) follows on from a 2022 Pentagon announcement that its then newly formed All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) had not found any evidence to suggest that aliens have visited Earth or crash-landed here.
Under the 2023 National Defense Authorization Act, AARO was required to issue a report to Congress detailing the governments historical record relating to "unidentified anomalous phenomena" (UAP) since 1945. - report from Reuters
(Production: Bernat Parera)

headline: 4 transgender Pinays tagged in Thai brawl flying home | ABS-CBN News
text:
MANILA — The four Pinoy transgender Filipinas involved in a brawl with Thai transgenders in Bangkok's red light district are already bound for Manila, the Philippine Embassy in Bangkok said. 
In a statement, the embassy said the Philippine Embassy paid the fines incurred by all four individuals. 
"The Embassy provided comprehensive assistance and support to our affected nationals. The fines ordered by the Bangkok Civil Court were paid and settled by the Philippine Government through the Philippine Embassy," it said in a statement.
READ: Thai police investigate 2 Filipino transgenders after brawl
In the same statement, the embassy also sounded the alarm on people taking advantage of the event to conduct scams guised as fundraising for their fines.
"We caution these concerned individuals from spreading misinformation or posting unsubstantiated information on social media," they said.
They also assured the public that this will not affect the relationship of both countries. 
Based on numerous reports, more than a hundred Thai transgender sex workers gathered outside the hotel where the Filipinos were checked in, due to a "turf war."
The Filipinos were identified as tourists but authorities in Thailand are investigating if they were illegally working there. They also called for assistance, citing threats to their life. 
— With a report by Zen Hernandez, ABS-CBN News
RELATED VIDEO

headline: 'Sitwasyon sa West PH Sea posibleng lumala' | ABS-CBN News
text: Naniniwala ang mga eksperto na kailangang maghanda ng Pilipinas sa posibleng paglala pa ng sitwasyon sa West Philippine Sea. Kasunod ito ng utos ni Chinese President Xi Jinping sa kanilang militar na paghandaan ang umano'y naval conflict para protektahan ang kanilang karapatan sa dagat. Nagpa-Patrol si Michael Delizo. TV Patrol, Sabado, 9 Marso 2024

headline: Dela Rosa sees nothing wrong with Quiboloy having firearms | ABS-CBN News
text:
Televangelist Apollo Quiboloy should not be questioned if there is truth to allegations that he has firearms, according his ally Senator Ronald “Bato” Dela Rosa.
Dela Rosa issued the statement when sought for reaction about a former Kingdom of Jesus Christ member’s claim that he saw Quiboloy going to his owned “Garden of Eden” bringing huge bags containing firearms.
The former follower of Quiboloy gave this information in one of the hearings of the Senate Committee on Women, Children, Family Relations and Gender Equality, which centers on the religious leader’s alleged human trafficking activities and reported abuses to his women and children followers.
Dela Rosa claimed “all” Mindanaoans are fond of guns and that is is not surprising if Quiboloy is one of them.
“Lahat ng taga-Mindanao ay mahilig sa baril. Karamihan sa mga tao sa Mindanao ay mahilig sa baril. Ano ang problem do’n kung may mga baril siya basta legal lang. Do you think si Pastor Quiboloy bibili ng baril na illegal? Wala naman tayong source ng illegal na baril,” Dela Rosa argued.
(All those who come from Mindanao are fond of guns. Majority of Mindanaoans are fond of guns So, what is the problem if the firearm is legal? Do you think Pastor Quiboloy will buy illegal firearms? We have no source of illegal firearms.)
Those questioning Quiboloy’s gun ownership issue can check from the PNP-Firearms and Explosives Office if the witness’s claim are true, the senator insisted.
“So tingnan na lang ninyo sa records sa FEO kung may mga record ba siya diyan na registered na baril,” the senator said.
(Just check the FEO records if he has registered firearms there)
Dela Rosa, former chief of the Philippine National Police, meanwhile, criticized the recommendation of other senators to ban civilians from owning semi-automatic rifle, on the belief that crimes will increase if the PNP will not recall such policy.
Civilians’ authority to own semi-automatic rifles is allowed under Republic Act 10591.
“The more you restrict the people from having this kind of firearms tapos kinakailangan nila, the more sila magba-violate sa batas. Pero kung bigyan mo sila ng avenue to make everything legal, then sino bang gustong gumawa ng illegal?” Dela Rosa said.
(The more you restrict people from having this kind of firearms when they need them, the more that they would violate the law. But if you will give them the avenue to make everything legal, then, who would want to be illegal?)

headline: PH diving sites presented to travelers at Vancouver travel show | ABS-CBN News
text:
The Philippine booth was a hub of activity as staff fielded questions about popular outdoor activities in the country during the recent outdoor adventure and travel show in Vancouver.
This is the fifth time for the Philippine Department of Tourism and the Consulate General in Vancouver to participate in the event.
Many came to the booth with questions about the best beaches and diving sites in the Philippines.
“A lot of people are becoming more and more interested in diving in the Philippines,” said Stephanie Hermoso of the DOT. “So, we're telling them that it's a very good destination.”
Allan Coo, country manager of Canada of Philippine Airlines, was also on hand to inform interested travelers that visiting the Philippines is now easier with their non-stop flights from Vancouver to Manila.
The Philippines was hailed last year as the world’s leading beach and dive destination and Manila, as the world’s leading city destination at the World Travel Awards.
Coo said the Philippines' dive sites have a number of additional features in its favor compared to other destinations in the world.
“We don't have a shortage of those places,” said Coo. “The Philippines is a good place to get their certification. It's affordable and the water is warm.
The Outdoor Adventure and Travel Show featured booths from various cities in North America as well as from different countries.
They aim to entice outdoorsy travelers with suggestions on what they can do and where they can go for their next trip.
Canadians Johnny and Morag Vantol, who have already visited some 35 countries around the world, said they are interested in checking out snorkeling in the Philippines.
“The Philippines looks like a place where we can do lots of exploration and swimming and snorkeling,” said Johnny. “I want to swim with the whales or whale sharks or whatever, but we like to stay on the surface. We don't like to go too deep.”
Infiniti Liveaboard is one of those offering trips to the Philippines’ fantastic dive sites.
The 39-meter yacht, owned by Canadian H.K. Bassi and her husband, will take 16 Canadian guests on a seven-day trip to the world-renowned Tubbataha Reef this month.
“It's every diver's bucket list item,” said Bassi. “If you haven't dived there, you should.”
Depending on the time of the year, Bassi said Infiniti Liveaboard also arranges trips to other remote dive sites in Cebu, Dumaguete, and Bohol in the Visayas.
PAL is celebrating its 83rd anniversary this year with a seat sale.
Coo said this is the perfect time for those who are ready to write off diving from their bucket list, to explore the richness of marine life in Philippine waters.

headline: Sunog sumiklab sa Pasay | ABS-CBN News
text: Sumiklab ang sunog sa Pasay City nitong Biyernes ng gabi. Mahigit isandaang indibidwal ang apektado na pansamantalang sumisilong sa basketball court. Nagpa-Patrol si Job Manahan, TV Patrol, Sabado, 9 Marso 2024.

headline: Plans to get aid into Gaza by sea gain pace as airdrop mishap kills 5 | ABS-CBN News
text:
PALESTINIAN TERRITORIES — An international effort is gathering pace to get desperately needed humanitarian relief into Gaza by sea in a bid to counter overland access restrictions blamed on Israel as it battles Hamas militants.
The dire conditions more than five months into the war have led some countries to airdrop food and other assistance over the besieged Gaza Strip, but a parachute malfunction turned the latest operation lethal on Friday.
Five Palestinians were killed and 10 wounded north of the coastal Al-Shati refugee camp, said Mohammed al-Sheikh, emergency room head nurse at Gaza City's Al-Shifa hospital.
READ: Biden says Gaza ceasefire by Ramadan 'looking tough'
A witness told AFP he and his brother followed the parachuted aid in the hope of getting "a bag of flour".
"Then, all of a sudden, the parachute didn't open and fell down like a rocket," hitting a house, said Mohammed al-Ghoul.
Jordanian and US military officials denied that aircraft from either country caused the fatalities.
"We express sympathies to the families of those who were killed," the US Central Command said in a statement.
"Contrary to some reports, this was not the result of US airdrops."
Belgium, Egypt, France and the Netherlands were also involved in the airdrop.



In the Cypriot port of Larnaca, European Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen expressed hope that a maritime corridor could open this Sunday, although details remained unclear.
She said a "pilot operation" was to be launched on Friday, aided by the United Arab Emirates which secured "the first of many shipments of goods to the people of Gaza".
US President Joe Biden said in Thursday's State of the Union address that the US military would build a "temporary pier" off Gaza's coast to bring in aid.
READ: Israel army warns Gaza war will continue throughout 2024
He told reporters on Friday that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu must allow in more aid, a day after he warned Israeli leaders against using aid as "a bargaining chip".
The United Nations has repeatedly warned of looming famine in the Gaza Strip, which is under Israeli siege following the October 7 attack by Hamas that triggered the war.
UN agencies have urged increased overland access, insisting that air or sea delivery was ineffective.
Biden acknowledged that hopes for a new truce deal before Ramadan, the Muslim fasting month that could begin on Sunday depending on the lunar calendar, were "looking tough".
'No compromise'
Hamas's unprecedented October attack on southern Israel resulted in about 1,160 deaths, most of them civilians, according to Israeli official figures.
Israel has responded with a relentless offensive that the health ministry in Hamas-run Gaza said has killed at least 30,878 people, mostly women and children.
Hamas militants also took about 250 hostages, some of whom were released during a week-long truce in November. Israel believes 99 hostages remain alive in Gaza and that 31 have died.
After a week of talks with mediators in Cairo failed to produce a breakthrough, Hamas's armed wing said it would not agree to a hostage-prisoner exchange without the withdrawal of Israeli forces.
Ezzedine al-Qassam Brigades spokesman Abu Obeida said "there is no compromise on this".
Hamas negotiators left Cairo to consult with the movement's leadership in Qatar but US officials denied the negotiations had broken down.
"The ball is in their court," US Secretary of State Antony Blinken said in Washington.
No US troops in Gaza
Israel, which withdrew from Gaza in 2005 but has maintained control over its airspace and territorial waters, said it "welcomes" the planned maritime corridor.
There are no functioning ports in Gaza and officials did not say where initial shipments would go, whether they would be subject to inspection by Israel, or who would distribute aid.
The Pentagon said on Friday the plan to establish the "Joint Logistics Over-the-Shore" port would take up to 60 days and likely involve up to 1,000 US personnel.
Once established, it "could provide more than two million meals to the citizens of Gaza per day", Pentagon spokesman Major General Pat Ryder told reporters in Washington.
The pier would allow ships to transfer cargo to smaller vessels to transport and offload onto a temporary causeway for delivery to Gaza, said Ryder, who repeated Biden's promise that there would be no US troops on the ground in Gaza.
US officials said the plan builds on the maritime aid corridor proposed by Cyprus — the closest European Union member to Gaza.
However, Michael Fakhri, the UN special rapporteur on the right to food, said Washington's "absurd" pier proposal would not "prevent starvation and famine by any definition".
A Biden administration official said Israel has agreed to open a new land crossing that would "allow for aid to flow directly to the population in northern Gaza", starting "over the coming week".
British foreign minister David Cameron said "we need 500 trucks a day or more going into Gaza", but the past five days have averaged just 123.
Cameron also called on Israel to ensure the "full resumption" of water and electricity supplies.
Desperate residents in Gaza's north have swarmed the aid trucks that have made it into the territory.
More than 100 Palestinians were killed on February 29 when Israeli forces opened fire on crowds scrambling for aid from a convoy in north Gaza, according to Gaza's health ministry.
Israel's military said on Friday its initial investigation found troops "fired precisely" at suspects who posed a threat to them.
Roughly 1.5 million Palestinians have sought refuge in Rafah, in Gaza's far south, but there, too, they are not safe.
At the city's Al-Najjar hospital, a man held the body of a child killed in a bombardment, shrouded in a blood-soaked white cloth.
"We do not feel the joy of Ramadan approaching," said displaced Palestinian woman Nevin al-Siksek.
© Agence France-Presse

headline: Biden says Gaza ceasefire by Ramadan 'looking tough' | ABS-CBN News
text:
ROSE VALLEY, United States — US President Joe Biden warned Friday that it would be "tough" to secure a ceasefire between Israel and Hamas by the Muslim holy month of Ramadan.
"It's looking tough," Biden told reporters when asked if a deal to halt the five-month-old conflict could be achieved by Ramadan, which is due to start as early as Sunday depending on the sighting of the moon.
Biden added that "I sure am" worried about the possibility of violence in Israeli-annexed east Jerusalem as Ramadan approaches, adding to a similar warning earlier this week.
READ: US senators race to avert government partial shutdown
Hamas's armed wing on Friday urged supporters to mobilize towards the Al-Aqsa mosque compound in east Jerusalem, a flashpoint for violence during Ramadan in past years.
It also said there would be no compromise on the movement's demand that Israel withdraw from Gaza to secure the release of hostages seized by Hamas.
The Israel-Hamas war was triggered by the Palestinian militants' unprecedented attack on southern Israel on October 7.
WATCH: Israeli airstrikes pound Gaza
© Agence France-Presse

headline: China's sexual minorities suffering violence in 'conversion therapy' | ABS-CBN News
text:
BEIJING — As gender diversity spreads around the world, the position of sexual minorities in Chinese society is deteriorating, with many sent to correctional facilities where they undergo brutal treatment in the name of "conversion therapy."
The accusation comes from a group of victims and their supporters who in recent interviews revealed that there are some 100 such facilities in the country, including hospitals and private institutions. They say inmates are subject to beatings and other violent acts to attempt to "correct" their sexual orientation or gender identities.
READ: LGBT group urges Marcos to certify SOGIE Equality Bill urgent
While prejudice based on conservative ideas pervasive in Chinese society plays its part, one expert said discrimination against lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and queer people is being spurred on by the Chinese leadership under President Xi Jinping, who see respecting the rights of sexual minorities as a "Western value."
The leadership "fears that any human rights activism by LGBTQ and other sexual minorities would lead to criticism of the system," Tomoko Ako, a University of Tokyo Graduate School professor specializing in contemporary China, told Kyodo News in a recent interview.
Many of the inpatients at facilities in Shanghai and other cities and provinces such as Hebei, Hubei and Sichuan are young people in their pre-teens, teens and 20s who say they were more or less forcibly brought to facilities by family members.
Even when they are able to escape for a short while, they are often brought back by police.
In what has become a rampant abusive practice, officers at facilities tell inmates that they are "sickening and useless," beat them and have them strip naked in front of others. The victims say some of them have also been sexually abused by officers at the facilities.
With a look of torment, a 23-year-old university student known as Reisen, who prefers the pronouns "they and their," recalled the "conversion therapy" they experienced last summer after being tricked into taking a ride with their father.
Reisen grew up as the eldest son of a family in Kunming, Yunnan Province, in the south. But around middle school, Reisen felt "different to other classmates."
"I noticed I had different feelings toward the opposite sex and my own subjects of interest," Reisen said, adding, "I was afraid to tell my parents about this."



Last August in Chengdu, Sichuan Province, Reisen was convinced by their father to take a drive with him to collect money for school, but when the pair arrived at their destination, it was a facility "for correction" adorned with military designs.
Aware that his "son" was a member of a sexual minority, the father forced Reisen to go through the admission process. Reisen's shoulder-length hair was sheared into a short crop at the facility, where the inmates were taught traditional Chinese values. Reisen also witnessed the sexual assault of a girl — a young teen or perhaps even younger — by an instructor who was a retired veteran.
In mid-September, with the support of a college professor and classmates, Reisen managed to leave the facility. It was "hell," Reisen said, adding, "The only emotion I have for my father now is hatred."
Having lost their place in Chinese society, large numbers of members of sexual minorities are now fleeing abroad.
Molly, 24, moved to Finland from China in 2022 and now has a research job there. Born male, Molly, who also prefers the pronouns "they and their," identifies more as a female than a male but says they do not like to be categorized as either.
Molly was forcibly brought to a facility — at their father's behest — seven years ago.
"I felt like I was having a mental collapse," Molly said in an online interview with Kyodo News, adding they became a victim of sexual abuse at the facility.
Inmates at the correctional facilities are denied access to the outside world with their smartphones and computers confiscated. Apart from their hair cut short, they go through tough military-like training and attend classes on traditional gender roles of "men who work and women who give birth."
Homosexuality used to be considered a mental disorder and was once outlawed in China. Patients were subjected to electric shock "correction" treatments, provoking criticism from international human rights organizations.
Homosexuality became legal in 1997 and was removed from the mental illness category in 2001, but the situation has deteriorated again in recent years.
In May last year, the Beijing LGBT Center discontinued its activities for protection of sexual minorities' human rights, ostensibly under pressure from Chinese authorities.
According to one estimate, China has an LGBT population of 75 million. "Thousands of them are considered victims of violence, but we don't know the exact number," said a member of a Chinese support group.
"Not only sexual minorities but also members of support groups are under pressure, subject to summons by the police and surveillance," noted Ako, the University of Tokyo Graduate School professor.
She said that Xi's leadership uses its dismissal of the concept of the rights of sexual minorities as a Western value in its propaganda, and that "people have been widely influenced, including the parental generation."
For people like Molly, living in such a society is no longer a viable option. "I don't have a place in my homeland. It's too dangerous to return there," Molly said.
Ako suggested giving a voice to sexual minorities is needed more than ever. "It is precisely in China, where speech is controlled, that it is important to communicate from the perspective of minorities," she said.

headline: Truck na may dalang mantika naaksidente sa SLEX | ABS-CBN News
text: Isang truck na kargado ng mantika ang naaksidente sa sa SLEX. Dahil dito ilang motorsita rin ang sumemplang sa dulas ng kalsada. Nagpa-Patrol si Izzy Lee. TV Patrol, Sabado, 9 Marso 2024

headline: Israeli strike on Rafah tower displaces around 300 families | ABS-CBN News
text:
Gazan residents of Rafah inspected the damages of a residential tower on Saturday (March 9) while some sat among their belongings in the rubble of al-Masri Tower where around 300 families were sheltering.
The Israeli army struck the residential area overnight displacing scores of people, some of whom were already displaced. There were no initial reports of casualties.
The residents said they had received a 30 minute advance warning of the attack and were not in the building at the time of the bombing, but added it was too short a time to evacuate, people panicked and chaos ensued.
"They told us to evacuate the tower within half an hour... People were startled, running down the stairs, some fell, it was chaos," said Mohammad al-Nabrees, a displaced Gazan from Khan Younis, who was sheltering for the time being in al-Masri tower.
At least 30,878 Palestinians have been killed and 72,402 have been wounded, the Gaza health ministry said in a statement on Friday, in Israel's military offensive on Gaza launched after Hamas' Oct. 7 attack on southern Israel, in which Israel said 1,200 people were killed and 253 abducted.
Swathes of the Gaza Strip have been laid to waste and nearly the entire population has been made homeless. -- Report from Reuters
(Production: Bassam Masoud, Nuha Sharaf)

headline: Marcos Jr. tutulak pa-Europa sa Lunes | ABS-CBN News
text: Babyaheng Europa sa Lunes si Pangulong Ferdinand Marcos Jr., para sa kanyang pagbisita sa Germany at Czech Republic. Layon ng kanyang biyahe ang mapalakas ang relasyon ng Pilipinas sa dalwang bansa. Nagpa-Patrol si Pia Gutierrez. TV Patrol, Sabado, 9 Marso 2024

headline: US-led coalition shoots down 15 Yemen rebel drones | ABS-CBN News
text:
A Houthi supporter holds up a mock drone in front of a banner of top Houthi leader Abdul-Malik al-Houthi during a protest against the US and Israel and in support of Palestinians, in Sana'a, Yemen, on March 1, 2024. Yahya Arhab, EPA-EFE 

US and allied forces shot down 15 one-way attack drones fired by Iran-backed Yemeni rebels into the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, the US military said on Saturday.
It was one of the Huthis' largest attacks since they began in November a campaign of drone and missile strikes against ships in the Red Sea area, vital for world trade, in professed solidarity with Palestinians during Israel's war against Hamas militants in the Gaza Strip.
The US Central Command, or CENTCOM, said the "large-scale" Huthi attack occurred before dawn into the Red Sea and adjacent Gulf of Aden.
CENTCOM and coalition forces determined that the drones "presented an imminent threat to merchant vessels, US Navy and coalition ships in the region".
It added, in a post on social media platform X, that "US Navy vessels and aircraft along with multiple coalition navy ships and aircraft shot down 15" of the drones.
"These actions are taken to protect freedom of navigation and make international waters safer and more secure."
On January 9, US and British forces shot down 18 drones and three missiles fired by the rebels toward ships in the Red Sea, the US military said at the time.
Britain said it was the largest attack to that point by the Huthis.
The United States in December announced a maritime security initiative to protect Red Sea shipping from Huthi attacks, which have forced commercial vessels to divert from the route that normally carries 12 percent of global trade.
Since January the United States and Britain have also launched repeated strikes on Huthi targets in Yemen in response to the ship attacks, but the rebels have continued to attack merchant vessels and have also targeted American and British ships.
